title: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly: Watermarks, Transferable Attacks and Adversarial Defenses

reviews.summary: The paper formalizes backdoor-based watermarks and adversarial defenses as interactive protocols, and introduces a third notion—transferable attacks—capturing indistinguishable, distributional queries that fool all efficient defenders. The main theorem (via a zero-sum game argument) asserts that for any efficiently learnable task, at least one of: a watermark, an adversarial defense, or a transferable attack must exist. The authors further (i) construct a transferable attack using fully homomorphic encryption on a mixed “clear/encrypted” distribution and (ii) show that the existence of transferable attacks implies EFID pairs (and hence standard cryptographic primitives), while also providing instantiations of defenses and watermarks for hypothesis classes of bounded VC-dimension.
strengths: Unifies watermarking and adversarial robustness under a common interactive-protocol lens and identifies a trichotomy (watermark/defense/transferable attack).
Establishes an intriguing link between transferable attacks and cryptography, with both a construction (via FHE) and an implication (transferable attacks ⇒ EFID pairs ⇒ PRGs).
Adapts large zero-sum game techniques (Lipton–Young) to argue the existence of one of the three mechanisms, a nontrivial idea that conceptually bridges two previously separate literatures.
Provides new existence results for bounded VC-dimension tasks: robust defenses against arbitrary (even unbounded) attackers and an example watermark against very fast adversaries.
The work is theoretical; theorems, reductions, and constructions are the right tools here. Within that scope, the claims are structured with formal statements (albeit much deferred to the appendix).
The high-level motivations and the interplay between the three notions are explained clearly and intuitively, with helpful figures and concrete narratives (Alice/Bob).
The cryptographic construction is motivated step-by-step, starting from a familiar geometric learning task.

Provides a principled map of possibilities for adversarial testing vs. watermark detection, with practical takeaways for AI safety: either watermarking or detectable defenses should prevail under broad conditions, unless the task hides cryptographic hardness.
The cryptographic equivalence result clarifies when “undetectable” adversarial testing can be expected in practice.
weaknesses: The main trichotomy result appears largely non-constructive and rests on game-theoretic existence coupled with small-support equilibrium arguments; algorithmic efficiency and how the time exponents (e.g., T1/√log T, O(T)) concretely arise need clearer justification in the main text.
The FHE-based transferable attack is built on a contrived distribution that mixes clear and encrypted samples—a powerful proof-of-existence, but arguably far from most ML practice.

Several core definitions (watermark “uniqueness,” “unremovability,” indistinguishability thresholds) are only informally specified in the main body, making it hard to audit edge cases without the appendices.

No empirical case studies or toy simulations demonstrating how the proposed defense/watermark protocols behave on natural tasks; while not required for a theory paper, even small-scale illustrations could strengthen intuition and relevance.

The “almost every task” phrasing is potentially misleading—Theorem 1 actually quantifies over all efficiently learnable tasks; the caveat seems to be about excluding tasks without efficient learners or those with cryptographic structure. This should be reconciled explicitly.

The watermark “verifiability” discussion acknowledges needing an external oracle or stronger gaps; this is important, but the current exposition leaves the practical detection criterion somewhat underspecified.
Some repetition and inconsistencies in the related work section (e.g., duplicated text about attacker control of the distribution).

The comparison to Christiano et al. (2024) is promising but remains informal; a more formal side-by-side of assumptions (who chooses the distribution/trigger), resources, and guarantees would benefit readers.
Additional discussion of impossibility/possibility frontiers in watermarking for generative models and their relationship to the current discriminative setting would add useful context.
reviews.questions:
1. Can you provide a more explicit, constructive description (in the main text) of how BNASH is found/represented and how BDEFENSE simulates it efficiently to achieve the claimed time bounds (especially the T and T1/√log T terms)?
2. How sensitive is Theorem 1 to the exact definitions of indistinguishability and the advantage thresholds? Can you state the quantitative dependence on ε, q, and advantage δ in the main theorem?
3. The FHE-based transferable attack relies on a mixed clear/encrypted distribution. Is there a variant that avoids mixing modes (e.g., entirely clear data) under weaker cryptographic assumptions, or can you argue a lower bound ruling that out?
4. The watermark “uniqueness” property hinges on resource gaps (TB < TA). In realistic settings where independent developers have TA-like resources, how would your verifiability test avoid false accusations? Can you formalize the “third-party oracle” assumption further?
5. For Lemma 1, could you sketch the concrete algorithm in your no-rejection, indistinguishability-required setting, and provide explicit sample/time complexity, highlighting differences from Goldwasser et al. (2020)?
6. The “almost every task” phrasing seems stronger than Theorem 1 (which quantifies over efficiently learnable tasks). Could you clarify this wording, and precisely delineate the set of excluded tasks?
reviews.assessment:
This is an ambitious and thought-provoking theory paper that unifies watermarking and adversarial defenses and adds a third, cryptography-linked pillar—transferable attacks—leading to a clean trichotomy. The connection to EFID/PRGs is elegant and conceptually valuable, and the bounded VC-dimension instantiations help ground the framework. That said, the main theorem is largely non-constructive and relies on game-theoretic existence with delicate time-parameter claims that deserve clearer exposition in the main text. The cryptographic construction, while insightful, uses an artificial mixed distribution that limits immediate practical resonance. Definitions and verification criteria (especially for watermark “uniqueness” and defense detectability) would benefit from more precise, quantitative statements up front, and the comparison to closely related work could be tightened.

On balance, I find the conceptual contribution meaningful for ICLR’s theory and safety communities. If the authors can strengthen the main-text proofs/algorithms for time bounds, clarify definitions and verifiability, and sharpen the comparison to Christiano et al. (2024) and robust learning works, this would be a valuable addition. I lean toward a weak accept for its unifying perspective and cryptographic boundary insights.
